name: French Defense
description: A solid, strategic defense where Black builds a pawn chain and fights for counterplay against White's center. Known for its closed, positional character.

moves.commentary: King's pawn opening.
The French Defense — Black prepares d5, building a solid pawn chain.
White claims the full center with two pawns.
Challenges e4 directly. The tension d5 vs e4 defines the French.
Defends e4 and develops the knight.
The Winawer! Pins the knight and puts immediate pressure on e4. The most dynamic French variation.
White advances, gaining space but creating a fixed pawn chain. The center closes.
Black attacks the base of White's chain at d4. Classic French strategy — undermine the chain.
Forces the bishop to declare its intentions. Will it capture or retreat?
Black gives up the bishop pair for structural damage. White gets doubled pawns.
Recaptures. White has the bishop pair but a shattered queenside pawn structure.
Develops the knight behind the pawn chain, heading for f5 or g6.
White aggressively targets g7, exploiting Black's dark-square weakness.
Defends g7 while putting pressure on the e5 pawn. Typical Winawer counterplay.
King's pawn.
French Defense setup.
Full center.
Challenges e4.
Defends e4.
The Classical French — develops the knight and maintains pressure on e4. More traditional than the Winawer.
Pins the knight! Creates the classic dilemma — if Black plays Be7, White exchanges and weakens the kingside pawns.
Unpins the knight. After the exchange on f6, Black will decide between structural integrity and activity.
Advances and gains space. The knight on f6 is driven back.
The knight retreats, keeping the option to pressure e5 from c5 or f6 later.
White exchanges, eliminating the defender of the dark squares.
Recaptures with the queen, maintaining flexibility.
Supports the e5 pawn and gains kingside space. White aims for a gradual kingside attack.
Black castles and prepares the typical c5 break and queenside play.
Develops the last knight. White has space; Black has a solid position and counterplay targets.
King's pawn.
French Defense.
Claims the center.
Challenges e4.
The Tarrasch! Defends e4 without blocking the c-pawn. Avoids the Winawer pin but develops more passively.
Develops and attacks e4.
Advances, gaining space and driving back the knight.
Knight retreats, preparing c5 to attack the pawn chain.
Develops the bishop to its best square, eyeing the kingside.
The classic French break — attacks the base of White's pawn chain.
Supports d4. White wants to maintain the chain.
Develops and pressures d4 further.
Develops the knight without blocking the bishop. Prepares to support the center.
Black opens lines and challenges White's structure.
Recaptures, maintaining the pawn chain. A typical French middlegame.
King's pawn.
French Defense.
Full center.
Challenges e4.
The Advance Variation — White pushes immediately, gaining space but fixing the pawn structure early.
Attacks the base of the pawn chain. Fundamental French strategy.
Supports d4 solidly. White builds a strong but rigid center.
Develops and pressures d4.
Develops the knight and supports d4.
Puts pressure on both b2 and d4. A common idea in the Advance French.
Develops solidly, preparing to castle.
An unusual-looking move but the knight heads to f5 to pressure d4 and e3.
White trades to prevent the knight reaching f5.
Black accepts doubled pawns but gains the open g-file for attacking chances.
